text:
Government of India 
Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare 
Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare 
Weekly report/ Minutes of the Crop Weather Watch Group 
(CWWG) meeting as on 08.06.2026
[IMAGE page_01_img_1]

metadata.source_pdf: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 08.06.2026.pdf

source_pdf: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 08.06.2026.pdf

text:
Key Insights 
1. Kharif Season Sowing Status:  
 Kharif sowing data collection has started from 05th June, 2026 and the sowing is in preliminary 
stage. As on June 05 2026, the area sown under Kharif crops is 72.57 lakh ha (including 54.08 lakh 
ha in Sugarcane) as compared to 74.61 lakh ha during the corresponding period of last year.   
 
2. Weather Update:  
a) 
Temperature: Maximum temperature is likely to be above normal in East India and many parts 
of Northeast India. 
b) Rainfall Distribution: From June 01– 08 June 2026, the All-India cumulative rainfall is 1% 
higher than its Long Period Average (LPA), with regional variations: (i) East & Northeast India: 
1%; (ii) Northwest India: (-) 6%; (iii) Central India: 17%; and South Peninsula: 0%. 
 A fresh western Disturbance is likely to affect Western Himalayan Region from 11th June. 
 Rainfall activity is likely in South India, many parts of Maharashtra, NE India, some parts of 
Central India, J&K, some parts of Punjab, some parts of Himachal Pradesh, and isolated places 
in Uttarakhand. 
 Rainfall is likely to be above normal in Kerala, some parts of South Interior Karnataka, 
Arunachal Pradesh, while it is below normal in coastal Karnataka, Konkan-Goa, Gujarat, 
Odisha, East India and the NMMT region.  
 Isolated heavy to very heavy rainfall (7-20cm) very likely over Kerala, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu 
and northeast India during next 7 days with isolated extremely heavy rainfall also likely over 
Karnataka during 8th – 10th June. 
 Gradual rise in maximum temperatures likely over Northwest India by 2-3°C till 10th June and 
Gradual fall by 2-4°C thereafter.  No significant change in maximum temperatures likely over 
the rest parts of country till 13th June 2026. 
 
 
3.  Inputs Situation 
 
a) Water Reservoir Status: As on 04 June 2026, the total live water storage in 166 important 
reservoirs across the country (monitored by CWC) is 54.726 billion cubic meter, which is 29.81% 
of total live storage capacity (183.565 BCM). The current year's storage is nearly 93.86 percent of 
last year's storage and 118.58 percent of the average storage of the last ten years during the 
corresponding period. The overall storage position is less than the corresponding period of last year 
in the country as a whole but is better than the Normal storage during the corresponding period. 
b) Groundwater Levels: In January, 2026, around 75.8% of the country's monitoring stations 
exhibited a depth to water level within 10 meters below ground level. Deeper water levels of more 
than 20 m are observed in around 13.5% of stations of the country covering mainly the western 
states, especially Western Rajasthan, Punjab, Haryana and Chandigarh.

metadata.source_pdf: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 08.06.2026.pdf

source_pdf: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 08.06.2026.pdf

text:
The deeper groundwater level in the states like Western Rajasthan, Punjab, Haryana and 
Chandigarh during January, 2026 has been significantly influenced by the over extraction of 
groundwater compared to the recharge in the area, indicating negative water balance.  
About 70% of the analyzed stations of the unconfined aquifer experienced a rise of water level in 
January, 2026 when compared to the decadal mean water level of 2016-2025.  
The Groundwater Drought Indices indicate that in January, 2026, groundwater conditions across 
India remained largely stable, with 90.30% of monitoring stations falling under normal condition in 
respect of Ground Water Drought. However, localized deficits were observed in several regions — 
notably in Arunachal Pradesh, Himachal Pradesh, Meghalaya, and Nagaland. 
 
 
 
c) Fertilizers: The fertilizer supply remains stable across all products. 
 
 
4. Pests & Diseases: The overall incidence of pests and diseases remained below the Economic Threshold 
Level.
[IMAGE page_03_img_1]

source_pdf: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 08.06.2026.pdf

text:
Details of each Segment 
1. 
RAINFALL 
 
During the week (28 May – 03 June 2026), lower rainfall was observed, as compared to Long Period 
Average (LPA) by 10%. Cumulative seasonal rainfall (01st March to 31 May 2026) is higher than 
LPA by 1%. 
 
     
    
       
 
Rainfall Forecast: 
 
Overall, rainfall is likely to be below normal over the entire country except Konkan & Goa, Madhya 
Maharashtra, Marathwada, Karnataka, Telangana, Andhra Pradesh, where it will be near normal to 
above normal during the week. 
  
  
TEMPERATURE: MIN & MAX 
 
Week 1 (05 June  - 11 June 2026):  
 
Gradual fall by 2-4°C in maximum temperatures likely  over many parts of Northwest India during 
04th -06th June and gradual rise by 3-5°C during 07th-09th June. 
 
No significant change in maximum temperatures likely over East India till 10th June except Bihar 
where rise in maximum temperature by 2-3°C till 06th June.
[IMAGE page_05_img_1]
[IMAGE page_05_img_2]
[IMAGE page_05_img_3]

metadata.source_pdf: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 08.06.2026.pdf

source_pdf: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 08.06.2026.pdf

text:
Week 2 (12 June  - 18 June 2026):  
 Maximum temperatures is likely to be above normal NW India, East India, NE India, Central 
India and Gujarat.  
 Low probability of Heatwave conditions likely over parts of northwest India (Uttar Pradesh) and parts 
of East India (Bihar, Jharkhand, Odisha, Gangetic West Bengal & Chhattisgarh) and adjoining central 
India (Madhya Pradesh) on some days of the week. 
 Above-normal (+1.6 to +3.0°C) to appreciably above-normal (+3.1 to +5.0°C) maximum 
temperatures are likely in parts of East India and the plains of northwest India, and above-normal 
maximum temperatures (+1.6 to +3.0°C) are likely over parts of northeast India. Near-normal weekly 
maximum temperatures (-1.5 to +1.5°C) are likely across the rest of the country during the week.
[IMAGE page_06_img_1]
[IMAGE page_06_img_2]

metadata.source_pdf: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 08.06.2026.pdf

source_pdf: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 08.06.2026.pdf

text:
Advance of Southwest Monsoon 2026: 
 
 The Northern Limit of Monsoon passes through 17.5°N/60°E, 17.5°N/65°E, 17°N/70°E, Devgad, 
Koppal, Ananthapuramu, Chennai, 15.5°N/85°E, 22°N/90.0°E, Kailashahar, Lamding, North 
Lakhimpur and 29°N/95°E. 
 
 Conditions are favourable for further advance of southwest monsoon into some more parts of 
central Arabian Sea, Maharashtra, Karnataka, some parts of Telangana, some more parts of Andhra 
Pradesh, remaining parts of Tamil Nadu & southwest Bay of Bengal and some more parts of 
westcentral & northwest Bay of Bengal, some parts of Chhattisgarh & Odisha, remaining parts of 
Northeastern states and some parts of Sub-Himalayan West Bengal & Sikkim during next 3-4 
days.
[IMAGE page_07_img_1]

  CROPS 
 
Kharif Crops - Area coverage 
 
Sugarcane: About 54.08 lakh ha area coverage under Sugarcane has been reported, which is the same as 
the area reported during the corresponding period of last year (54.08 lakh ha).  
 
Cotton: About 7.51 lakh ha area coverage has been reported compared to 9.72 lakh ha during the 
corresponding period of last year. 
 
Jute: About 6.18 lakh ha area coverage has been reported compared to 6.08 lakh ha during the 
corresponding period of last year.

text:
3. 
RESERVOIR STORAGE  
 
As on 04 June, 2026, the current live storage in 166 reservoirs was 54.726 BCM (29.81%) of total live 
capacity of 183.565 BCM. 
 125 reservoirs having storage more than 80% of normal storage. 
 16 reservoirs having storage 51% to 80%. 
 25 reservoirs having storage upto 50%. 
 
      
Source: CWC

text:
4. REMOTE SENSING BASED SOIL MOISTURE OBSERVATION 
 
Soil Moisture Observation  
 
Soil moisture during 30 May – 05 June 2026 is similar to the average of the past 10 years in 
major parts of Rajasthan, Gujarat, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, Telangana, Northern and 
Central Karnataka, Punjab, Bihar, Jharkhand, Uttarakhand, Uttar Pradesh H.P., Tamil Nadu and 
J & K. 
 
Soil moisture is better than the average of the past 10 years in parts of Northeastern parts of 
Bihar, Northern West Bengal and Ladakh. 
 
It was lower than average of past 10 years in pockets of Odisha, Chhattisgarh, Haryana, 
Southern Karnataka, Western Maharashtra, Southern West Bengal, parts of Andhra Pradesh and 
in the Northeastern parts of Country 
 
 
 
.
[IMAGE page_10_img_1]

text:
Government of India 
Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare 
Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare 
Weekly report/ Minutes of the Crop Weather Watch Group 
(CWWG) meeting as on 01.06.2026
[IMAGE page_01_img_1]

metadata.source_pdf: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 01.06.2026.pdf

source_pdf: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 01.06.2026.pdf

text:
Key Insights 
1. Rabi Season harvest and Summer Season Sowing Status:  
 As per 3rd Advance Estimates 2025-26, the area sown under the summer crops is 87.26 lakh ha as 
compared to 83.91 lakh ha during the corresponding period of last year. As compared to previous year, 
Rice area declined by 0.65 lakh ha whereas, Maize area increased by 2.08 lakh ha, Groundnut by 1.03 
lakh ha, Urad by 0.76 lakh ha and Shree Anna by 0.70 lakh ha.  
 
 As on 01.06.2026, rabi crops harvesting has been completed in all states. 
 
2. Weather Update:  
a) Temperature: Maximum temperature is likely to be below normal in most of country except in 
Northeast India. 
 
b) Rainfall Distribution: From March 01– May 31 2026, the All-India cumulative rainfall is 1% 
higher than its Long Period Average (LPA), with regional variations: (i) East & Northeast India: 
1%; (ii) Northwest India: (-) 6%; (iii) Central India: 17%; and South Peninsula: 0%. 
 The Western Disturbance as a cyclonic circulation over northwest Uttar Pradesh & neighbourhood 
between 3.1 & 7.6 km above mean sea level with a trough aloft in middle & upper tropospheric 
westerlies with its axis at 5.8 km above mean sea level roughly along Long 78°E to the north of 
Lat 20°N persists.  
 Isolated heavy rainfall likely over Northeast India and South Peninsular India during the week. 
 Moderate to Severe Thunderstorm activity with squally wind speed upto 90kmph and isolated 
hailstorm likely over Northwest, Central & adjoining East India on 31st May. Isolated Hailstorm 
activity likely over Odisha, West Madhya Pradesh and Madhya Maharashtra on 01st June. 
 Normal to above normal rainfall is expected over central and northwest India. 
 Heat wave condition likely to prevail over Bihar during 04th–06th June.  
 
 
3.  Inputs Situation 
 
a) Water Reservoir Status: As on 29 May 2026, the total live water storage in 166 important 
reservoirs across the country (monitored by CWC) is 56.300 billion cubic meter, which is 30.67% 
of total live storage capacity (183.565 BCM). The current year's storage is nearly 100.73 percent of 
last year's storage and 119.11 percent of the average storage of the last ten years during the 
corresponding period. The overall storage position is better than the corresponding period of last 
year in the country as a whole and is also better than the Normal storage during the corresponding 
period.   
b) Groundwater Levels: In January, 2026, around 75.8% of the country's monitoring stations 
exhibited a depth to water level within 10 meters below ground level. Deeper water levels of more 
than 20 m are observed in around 13.5% of stations of the country covering mainly the western 
states, especially Western Rajasthan, Punjab, Haryana and Chandigarh.

metadata.source_pdf: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 01.06.2026.pdf

source_pdf: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 01.06.2026.pdf

text:
The deeper groundwater level in the states like Western Rajasthan, Punjab, Haryana and 
Chandigarh during January, 2026 has been significantly influenced by the over extraction of 
groundwater compared to the recharge in the area, indicating negative water balance.  
About 70% of the analyzed stations of the unconfined aquifer experienced a rise of water level in 
January, 2026 when compared to the decadal mean water level of 2016-2025.  
The Groundwater Drought Indices indicate that in January, 2026, groundwater conditions across 
India remained largely stable, with 90.30% of monitoring stations falling under normal condition in 
respect of Ground Water Drought. However, localized deficits were observed in several regions — 
notably in Arunachal Pradesh, Himachal Pradesh, Meghalaya, and Nagaland. 
 
 
 
c) Fertilizers: The fertilizer supply remains stable across all products. 
 
 
4. Pests & Diseases: The overall incidence of pests and diseases remained below the Economic Threshold 
Level. The monitoring of Cotton crop for Pink Ball Worm is progressing as per the protocol.
[IMAGE page_03_img_1]

metadata.source_pdf: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 01.06.2026.pdf

source_pdf: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 01.06.2026.pdf

text:
Details of each Segment 
1. 
RAINFALL 
 
During the week (21 May – 27 May 2026), lower rainfall was observed, as compared to Long Period 
Average (LPA) by 3%. Cumulative seasonal rainfall (01st March to 27 May 2026) is higher than 
LPA by 1%. 
 
     
    
       
 
Rainfall Forecast: 
• 
Normal Rainfall activity likely over central and Northeast India. Below normal rainfall is likely over 
south peninsular India and western Himalayan region. 
  
  
TEMPERATURE: MIN & MAX 
 
Week 1 (29 May – 04 June):  
 Maximum temperatures are very likely to be 5-6 Deg Celsius below normal over most parts of the 
country during the week except over North-Eastern States. 
 
Week 2 (05 June  - 11 June 2026):  
 Low probability of Heatwave conditions likely over some parts of northwest India (Rajasthan, south 
Punjab, south Haryana, south Uttar Pradesh) during some days of the week.
[IMAGE page_05_img_1]
[IMAGE page_05_img_2]
[IMAGE page_05_img_3]

metadata.source_pdf: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 01.06.2026.pdf

source_pdf: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 01.06.2026.pdf

text:
 Above-normal (+1.6 to +3.0°C) to appreciably above-normal (+3.1 to +5.0°C) maximum 
temperatures are likely over some parts of East India (Bihar, Gangetic West Bengal & Odisha). Near-
normal weekly maximum temperatures (-1.5 to +1.5°C) are likely across the rest of the country 
during the week. 
 Maximum temperatures are very likely to be 4-5 Deg Celsius below normal over most parts of the 
country during week 2 except many parts of Odisha, West Bengal, Bihar, and Arunachal 
Pradesh, West Rajasthan, Punjab. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Advance of Southwest Monsoon 2026: 
 
 The Northern Limit of Monsoon continues to pass through 10°N/60°E, 9°/65°E, 8.5°N/70°E, 
8°N/75°E, 7.8°N/78°E, 8°N/80°E, 10°N/80°E, 13°N/87°E, 16°N/90°E, 19°N/93°E and 22°N/97°E. 
 
 Conditions are favourable for further advance of southwest monsoon into some more parts of 
southwest & southeast Arabian Sea, Lakshadweep Islands, some parts of Kerala & Tamil Nadu,
[IMAGE page_06_img_1]
[IMAGE page_06_img_2]

metadata.source_pdf: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 01.06.2026.pdf

source_pdf: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 01.06.2026.pdf

text:
some more parts of southwest, westcentral, eastcentral & northeast Bay of Bengal, and remaining 
parts of southeast Bay of Bengal during next 3-4 days. 
 
 
Forecast for the 2026 Southwest Monsoon Rainfall over the Country as a Whole 
 
 Quantitatively, the southwest monsoon seasonal rainfall over the country as a whole is likely to be 
90% of the Long Period Average (LPA) with a model error of ±4%, indicating that below normal 
or less rainfall is most likely. 
 The long period average (LPA) of rainfall over the country is about 87 cm based on the data of 1971-
2020 
  The Probability forecast suggests 84% probability for below normal or less rainfall for the country as 
a whole against climatological probability of 33%.
[IMAGE page_07_img_1]
[IMAGE page_07_img_2]

text:
2. 
  CROPS 
 
Rabi Crops - Area coverage 
 
As per 3rd Advance Estimates 2025-26, about 116.3 percent of the normal area has been 
sown under the summer season crops. 
 
Rice: About 32.63 lakh ha area coverage under Summer Rice has been reported as 
compared to 33.28 lakh ha during the corresponding period of last year.  
 
Pulses: About 27.16 lakh ha area coverage has been reported compared to 27.07 lakh ha 
during the corresponding period of last year.  
 
Oilseeds: About 10.62 lakh ha area coverage has been reported compared to 9.51 lakh ha 
during the corresponding period of last year.

text:
3. 
RESERVOIR STORAGE  
As on 29 May, 2026, the current live storage in 166 reservoirs was 56.300 BCM (30.67%) of total live 
capacity of 183.565 BCM. 
 125 reservoirs having storage more than 80% of normal storage. 
 26 reservoirs having storage 51% to 80%. 
 15 reservoirs having storage upto 50%. 
 
      
 
Source: CWC

text:
5. 
REMOTE SENSING BASED SOIL MOISTURE OBSERVATION 
 
Soil Moisture Observation  
 
Soil moisture during 22 May – 28 May 2026 is similar to the average of the past 10 years in major parts 
of Rajasthan, Gujarat, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, Telangana, Northern and Central Karnataka, 
Kerala, Punjab, Bihar, Jharkhand, Uttarakhand, Uttar Pradesh H.P., Tamil Nadu and J & K. 
 
]Soil moisture is better than the average of the past 10 years in parts of Northeastern parts of Bihar, 
Northern West Bengal and Ladakh. 
 
It was lower than average of past 10 years in pockets of Odisha, Chhattisgarh, Haryana, Southern 
Karnataka, Southern West Bengal, parts of Andhra Pradesh and the Northeastern States. 
 
 
 
 
.
[IMAGE page_10_img_1]

9.   PROCUREMENT 
 
Central Pool Stock (As on 16.05.2026): Actual stock of Rice was 397.05 LMT against Buffer norm of 
135.80 LMT.  Actual stock of wheat was 465.10 LMT against Buffer Norm of 74.60 LMT. Therefore, 
Actual stock of Rice & Wheat together was 862.15 LMT against buffer norm of 210.40 LMT. 
o Buffer norm changes quarterly, above-mentioned norm is as on 1st April, it changes next on 
1st July and so on.
